title: Hodgkin Lymphoma
oneLiner: A curable B-cell lymphoma causing painless lymphadenopathy, often in young adults; mediastinal mass can cause life-threatening obstruction.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Superior Vena Cava Obstruction (SVCO):** Facial oedema, distended neck veins, stridor → immediately treat with high-flow O₂, dexamethasone, oncology review. **Single finding:** Unable to lie flat + facial plethora.
- **Airway compromise from large mediastinal mass:** Stridor, wheeze, orthopnoea → immediate CT thorax, avoid sedation, prepare for potential anaesthetic high-risk. **Single finding:** Peak expiratory flow <50% predicted or biphasic stridor.
- **Spinal cord compression (rare but missable):** Back pain + leg weakness/sensory level → urgent MRI spine + dexamethasone. **Single finding:** New bilateral leg weakness in a patient with known or suspected lymphoma.
sections.Presentation:
- Painless, rubbery, enlarged lymph node(s) – most commonly cervical or supraclavicular (60-80%)
- Mediastinal mass on CXR (often found incidentally in young adults)
- **B symptoms:** unexplained fever >38°C, drenching night sweats, unintentional weight loss >10% in 6 months
- Severe pruritus (sometimes precedes diagnosis by months)
- Alcohol-induced pain in involved nodes (classic but rare)
- Fatigue, malaise
- **Exam findings:** Palpate all node beds (cervical, axillary, inguinal); check for hepatosplenomegaly; look for signs of SVCO (facial puffiness, dilated chest veins); auscultate for stridor.
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Reactive lymphadenopathy (most common):** Usually <2 cm, tender, resolves with infection treatment – **splitter:** unilateral, >3 cm, or persistent >4 weeks requires biopsy.
- **Non-Hodgkin lymphoma (NHL):** Often more widespread, extranodal involvement, faster growth – **splitter:** B symptoms + mediastinal mass favours Hodgkin.
- **Metastatic carcinoma (e.g. head/neck, lung):** Hard, fixed, often older patient + smoking history – **splitter:** node matted to deep tissues.
- **Tuberculous lymphadenitis:** Often matted, sinus formation, positive IGRA/TST – **splitter:** cold abscess, fluctuant.
- **Infectious mononucleosis (EBV):** Sore throat, splenomegaly, positive Monospot – **splitter:** bilateral, acute fever.
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:** Pulse oximetry, ECG if SVCO (look for low voltage/tachycardia), flow-volume loop if stridor (if available). Urine pregnancy test in women of childbearing age (before chemotherapy).
- **Bloods:** FBC (anaemia, thrombocytopenia), ESR (elevated), LDH (prognostic), U&E, LFTs, albumin, HIV, hepatitis B/C serology (for treatment planning).
- **Imaging:**
    - **CXR:** First line – look for mediastinal widening, hilar adenopathy, pleural effusion.
    - **CT neck/chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast:** Staging – measure node size, spleen/liver involvement.
    - **PET-CT:** Essential for staging and response assessment (Deauville score: 1-5). Do *after* biopsy, not before.
- **Special tests:** **Excisional lymph node biopsy** (not FNA – insufficient material) – histology shows Reed-Sternberg cells, CD15+, CD30+. Immunohistochemistry confirms subtype (nodular sclerosis most common).
- **Never-miss test:** **Excisional biopsy of a suspicious node** – a core/fine needle aspirate risks false negative and delays diagnosis. If mediastinal mass, get urgent CT to assess airway.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
    - SVCO or stridor – **Call oncology + anaesthetics STAT** → High-flow O₂, raise head of bed, IV dexamethasone 8 mg stat, avoid sedatives, urgent CT thorax.
    - Suspected spinal cord compression – **Call neurosurgery/oncology** → IV dexamethasone 16 mg, urgent MRI whole spine.
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
    - Admit under haematology/oncology team.
    - Complete staging: PET-CT, bone marrow biopsy (if advanced stage or B symptoms).
    - IV fluids if dehydrated (due to fever/poor intake) – 0.9% saline 1L/8h, reassess.
    - Antiemetics if nausea (chemotherapy or large nodes).
    - Treat infections promptly – risk of immunocompromise (especially if splenic involvement).
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
    - Chemotherapy: ABVD (doxorubicin, bleomycin, vinblastine, dacarbazine) or escalated BEACOPP in advanced disease. Regimen selected by consultant.
    - ± Involved field radiotherapy (for bulky disease or early stage).
    - Fertility preservation counselling (sperm banking/egg retrieval) before starting treatment.
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
    - IV fluids: 0.9% NaCl at 80-100 mL/h if not eating (monitor for fluid overload if SVCO/cardiac risk).
    - VTE prophylaxis: Enoxaparin 40 mg SC daily (unless bleeding risk/platelets <50).
    - Diet: NBM only if stridor/airway risk; otherwise normal as tolerated.
    - Observations: 4-hourly NEWS2; if stridor or SVCO, continuous SpO₂.
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line chemotherapy (ABVD example – consultant prescribes, FY1 supports):**
    - Doxorubicin 25 mg/m² IV days 1 & 15
    - Bleomycin 10,000 U/m² IV days 1 & 15
    - Vinblastine 6 mg/m² IV days 1 & 15
    - Dacarbazine 375 mg/m² IV days 1 & 15
    - Cycle repeats every 28 days.
- **Antiemetics (for chemotherapy):** Ondansetron 8 mg IV/PO pre-chemo + dexamethasone 8 mg IV (has dual role). [check local protocol]
- **G-CSF (filgrastim):** 5 μg/kg SC daily if neutropenia (ANC <0.5) – [check local policy for dosing].
- **Dexamethasone (for SVCO/SC compression):** 8-16 mg IV stat, then 4-8 mg PO/IV QDS, taper.
- **Key interactions:** Avoid live vaccines (e.g. MMR, yellow fever) during treatment; bleomycin + high-flow O₂ → pneumonitis (keep FiO₂ <0.3 during anaesthesia).
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** Doxorubicin and vinblastine require dose adjustment with hepatic impairment (check bilirubin/transaminases). Bleomycin dose reduction if CrCl <30. [check local policy]
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Bleomycin** – giving high inspired oxygen (>0.3) during sedation/anaesthesia can cause fatal interstitial pneumonitis. **Must** document allergy-like warning: "Bleomycin – avoid high FiO₂". Also, doxorubicin is vesicant – extravasation causes tissue necrosis; ensure good IV access.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
    - SVCO (from mediastinal mass)
    - Tumour lysis syndrome (rare in HL but possible with bulky disease; monitor K+, PO₄, Ca, urate)
    - Chemotherapy side effects: neutropenic sepsis, bleomycin pneumonitis, nausea/vomiting, alopecia, constipation (vinblastine)
- **Chronic:**
    - Secondary malignancies (lung, breast, AML) – risk persists for decades
    - Cardiotoxicity (doxorubicin cumulative dose >400 mg/m²; monitor echo)
    - Pulmonary fibrosis (bleomycin)
    - Infertility (especially if BEACOPP or pelvic radiotherapy)
    - Hypothyroidism (if neck radiation)
    - Fatigue and cognitive impairment
sections.Prognosis: - Excellent overall: >90% cure in early-stage, >80% in advanced-stage. Risk stratified by International Prognostic Score (IPS): albumin <40 g/L, Hb <105 g/L, male, age ≥45, stage IV, WBC ≥15×10⁹/L, lymphocyte <0.6×10⁹/L – each point worsens 5-year freedom from progression.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Always get an excisional node biopsy** – FNA is inadequate. If node is small, ultrasound-guided core can be acceptable, but discuss with pathologist.
- **Check peak expiratory flow (PEF) before any procedure or transfer in patients with mediastinal mass** – if PEF <50% predicted, discuss anaesthetics and consider tracheal stenting.
- **Refer all women of childbearing age for fertility preservation** before starting chemotherapy – time-sensitive.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Do not start steroids before biopsy** – steroids can partially lyse lymphoma and make diagnosis impossible (exception: emergency SVCO/SC compression).
- **Do not assume reactive if antibiotics given – nodes may shrink temporarily if coexisting infection, but Hodgkin will recur.**
- **Do not use high FiO₂ in patients who have received or will receive bleomycin** – this is a lifelong risk. Ensure anaesthetic notes and patient alert wristband.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:** Fever >38°C or rigors (neutropenic sepsis). Worsening breathlessness/hoarseness/stridor. Back pain with leg weakness. New jaundice or dark urine (tumour lysis or hepatitis B reactivation).
- **Follow-up:** Usually under haematology oncology; first PET-CT after 2 cycles. Plan: 3-6 monthly for 2 years, then annual. Long-term screening for secondary cancers.
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **NEWS2 ≥5** (especially if respiratory rate >24, SpO₂ <92%, or systolic BP <90) – sepsis? bleomycin pneumonitis? SVCO?
- **Lactate >2 mmol/L** – consider sepsis or tumour lysis.
- **GCS <15** or new focal neurology – exclude cord compression or cerebral involvement.
- **Stridor or threatened airway** – call anaesthetics + oncology immediately.
- **Neutropenic sepsis (ANC <0.5 + fever)** – start IV piperacillin-tazobactam per local policy, escalate to consultant.

rawMarkdown:
## Hodgkin Lymphoma
**One-liner:** A curable B-cell lymphoma causing painless lymphadenopathy, often in young adults; mediastinal mass can cause life-threatening obstruction.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Superior Vena Cava Obstruction (SVCO):** Facial oedema, distended neck veins, stridor → immediately treat with high-flow O₂, dexamethasone, oncology review. **Single finding:** Unable to lie flat + facial plethora.
- **Airway compromise from large mediastinal mass:** Stridor, wheeze, orthopnoea → immediate CT thorax, avoid sedation, prepare for potential anaesthetic high-risk. **Single finding:** Peak expiratory flow <50% predicted or biphasic stridor.
- **Spinal cord compression (rare but missable):** Back pain + leg weakness/sensory level → urgent MRI spine + dexamethasone. **Single finding:** New bilateral leg weakness in a patient with known or suspected lymphoma.

### Presentation
- Painless, rubbery, enlarged lymph node(s) – most commonly cervical or supraclavicular (60-80%)
- Mediastinal mass on CXR (often found incidentally in young adults)
- **B symptoms:** unexplained fever >38°C, drenching night sweats, unintentional weight loss >10% in 6 months
- Severe pruritus (sometimes precedes diagnosis by months)
- Alcohol-induced pain in involved nodes (classic but rare)
- Fatigue, malaise
- **Exam findings:** Palpate all node beds (cervical, axillary, inguinal); check for hepatosplenomegaly; look for signs of SVCO (facial puffiness, dilated chest veins); auscultate for stridor.

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Reactive lymphadenopathy (most common):** Usually <2 cm, tender, resolves with infection treatment – **splitter:** unilateral, >3 cm, or persistent >4 weeks requires biopsy.
- **Non-Hodgkin lymphoma (NHL):** Often more widespread, extranodal involvement, faster growth – **splitter:** B symptoms + mediastinal mass favours Hodgkin.
- **Metastatic carcinoma (e.g. head/neck, lung):** Hard, fixed, often older patient + smoking history – **splitter:** node matted to deep tissues.
- **Tuberculous lymphadenitis:** Often matted, sinus formation, positive IGRA/TST – **splitter:** cold abscess, fluctuant.
- **Infectious mononucleosis (EBV):** Sore throat, splenomegaly, positive Monospot – **splitter:** bilateral, acute fever.

### Investigations
- **Bedside:** Pulse oximetry, ECG if SVCO (look for low voltage/tachycardia), flow-volume loop if stridor (if available). Urine pregnancy test in women of childbearing age (before chemotherapy).
- **Bloods:** FBC (anaemia, thrombocytopenia), ESR (elevated), LDH (prognostic), U&E, LFTs, albumin, HIV, hepatitis B/C serology (for treatment planning).
- **Imaging:**
    - **CXR:** First line – look for mediastinal widening, hilar adenopathy, pleural effusion.
    - **CT neck/chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast:** Staging – measure node size, spleen/liver involvement.
    - **PET-CT:** Essential for staging and response assessment (Deauville score: 1-5). Do *after* biopsy, not before.
- **Special tests:** **Excisional lymph node biopsy** (not FNA – insufficient material) – histology shows Reed-Sternberg cells, CD15+, CD30+. Immunohistochemistry confirms subtype (nodular sclerosis most common).
- **Never-miss test:** **Excisional biopsy of a suspicious node** – a core/fine needle aspirate risks false negative and delays diagnosis. If mediastinal mass, get urgent CT to assess airway.

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
    - SVCO or stridor – **Call oncology + anaesthetics STAT** → High-flow O₂, raise head of bed, IV dexamethasone 8 mg stat, avoid sedatives, urgent CT thorax.
    - Suspected spinal cord compression – **Call neurosurgery/oncology** → IV dexamethasone 16 mg, urgent MRI whole spine.
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
    - Admit under haematology/oncology team.
    - Complete staging: PET-CT, bone marrow biopsy (if advanced stage or B symptoms).
    - IV fluids if dehydrated (due to fever/poor intake) – 0.9% saline 1L/8h, reassess.
    - Antiemetics if nausea (chemotherapy or large nodes).
    - Treat infections promptly – risk of immunocompromise (especially if splenic involvement).
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
    - Chemotherapy: ABVD (doxorubicin, bleomycin, vinblastine, dacarbazine) or escalated BEACOPP in advanced disease. Regimen selected by consultant.
    - ± Involved field radiotherapy (for bulky disease or early stage).
    - Fertility preservation counselling (sperm banking/egg retrieval) before starting treatment.
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
    - IV fluids: 0.9% NaCl at 80-100 mL/h if not eating (monitor for fluid overload if SVCO/cardiac risk).
    - VTE prophylaxis: Enoxaparin 40 mg SC daily (unless bleeding risk/platelets <50).
    - Diet: NBM only if stridor/airway risk; otherwise normal as tolerated.
    - Observations: 4-hourly NEWS2; if stridor or SVCO, continuous SpO₂.

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line chemotherapy (ABVD example – consultant prescribes, FY1 supports):**
    - Doxorubicin 25 mg/m² IV days 1 & 15
    - Bleomycin 10,000 U/m² IV days 1 & 15
    - Vinblastine 6 mg/m² IV days 1 & 15
    - Dacarbazine 375 mg/m² IV days 1 & 15
    - Cycle repeats every 28 days.
- **Antiemetics (for chemotherapy):** Ondansetron 8 mg IV/PO pre-chemo + dexamethasone 8 mg IV (has dual role). [check local protocol]
- **G-CSF (filgrastim):** 5 μg/kg SC daily if neutropenia (ANC <0.5) – [check local policy for dosing].
- **Dexamethasone (for SVCO/SC compression):** 8-16 mg IV stat, then 4-8 mg PO/IV QDS, taper.
- **Key interactions:** Avoid live vaccines (e.g. MMR, yellow fever) during treatment; bleomycin + high-flow O₂ → pneumonitis (keep FiO₂ <0.3 during anaesthesia).
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** Doxorubicin and vinblastine require dose adjustment with hepatic impairment (check bilirubin/transaminases). Bleomycin dose reduction if CrCl <30. [check local policy]
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Bleomycin** – giving high inspired oxygen (>0.3) during sedation/anaesthesia can cause fatal interstitial pneumonitis. **Must** document allergy-like warning: "Bleomycin – avoid high FiO₂". Also, doxorubicin is vesicant – extravasation causes tissue necrosis; ensure good IV access.

### Complications
- **Acute:**
    - SVCO (from mediastinal mass)
    - Tumour lysis syndrome (rare in HL but possible with bulky disease; monitor K+, PO₄, Ca, urate)
    - Chemotherapy side effects: neutropenic sepsis, bleomycin pneumonitis, nausea/vomiting, alopecia, constipation (vinblastine)
- **Chronic:**
    - Secondary malignancies (lung, breast, AML) – risk persists for decades
    - Cardiotoxicity (doxorubicin cumulative dose >400 mg/m²; monitor echo)
    - Pulmonary fibrosis (bleomycin)
    - Infertility (especially if BEACOPP or pelvic radiotherapy)
    - Hypothyroidism (if neck radiation)
    - Fatigue and cognitive impairment

### Prognosis
- Excellent overall: >90% cure in early-stage, >80% in advanced-stage. Risk stratified by International Prognostic Score (IPS): albumin <40 g/L, Hb <105 g/L, male, age ≥45, stage IV, WBC ≥15×10⁹/L, lymphocyte <0.6×10⁹/L – each point worsens 5-year freedom from progression.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Always get an excisional node biopsy** – FNA is inadequate. If node is small, ultrasound-guided core can be acceptable, but discuss with pathologist.
- **Check peak expiratory flow (PEF) before any procedure or transfer in patients with mediastinal mass** – if PEF <50% predicted, discuss anaesthetics and consider tracheal stenting.
- **Refer all women of childbearing age for fertility preservation** before starting chemotherapy – time-sensitive.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Do not start steroids before biopsy** – steroids can partially lyse lymphoma and make diagnosis impossible (exception: emergency SVCO/SC compression).
- **Do not assume reactive if antibiotics given – nodes may shrink temporarily if coexisting infection, but Hodgkin will recur.**
- **Do not use high FiO₂ in patients who have received or will receive bleomycin** – this is a lifelong risk. Ensure anaesthetic notes and patient alert wristband.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:** Fever >38°C or rigors (neutropenic sepsis). Worsening breathlessness/hoarseness/stridor. Back pain with leg weakness. New jaundice or dark urine (tumour lysis or hepatitis B reactivation).
- **Follow-up:** Usually under haematology oncology; first PET-CT after 2 cycles. Plan: 3-6 monthly for 2 years, then annual. Long-term screening for secondary cancers.

### Escalation Criteria
- **NEWS2 ≥5** (especially if respiratory rate >24, SpO₂ <92%, or systolic BP <90) – sepsis? bleomycin pneumonitis? SVCO?
- **Lactate >2 mmol/L** – consider sepsis or tumour lysis.
- **GCS <15** or new focal neurology – exclude cord compression or cerebral involvement.
- **Stridor or threatened airway** – call anaesthetics + oncology immediately.
- **Neutropenic sepsis (ANC <0.5 + fever)** – start IV piperacillin-tazobactam per local policy, escalate to consultant.
